title: To direct the Secretary of Defense to establish a compensation fund for military firefighters exposed to PFAS.
plain_summary: This bill tells the Defense Department to create a special fund within two years to help military firefighters who got sick from dangerous chemicals called PFAS while doing their jobs. The fund will pay for medical care and give money to firefighters or their families when the chemicals made them ill. If a firefighter dies from these illnesses, their spouse or other family members can still get payments from the fund. The bill assumes that military firefighters were exposed to these harmful chemicals if they worked at military bases where the chemicals were used.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

sections.title: Section 1 Compensation Fund for Military Firefighters
sections.text: The Secretary of Defense must create a program and fund called the Military Firefighters Compensation Fund within two years of this law being passed. This program will provide fair and timely payment to current and former military firefighters who got sick from being exposed to PFAS chemicals while doing their jobs. PFAS are man-made chemicals called per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances. The program will also pay the families of firefighters who have died. Military firefighters will be considered exposed to PFAS if they worked at a military base, National Guard facility, or former defense site during times when PFAS chemicals would have been present there, unless there is strong evidence proving otherwise. Current or former military firefighters, or their surviving family members if the firefighter has died, will receive money for disabilities or death caused by illnesses from their work exposure. If a military firefighter has died, payments will be made in a specific order. First to the surviving spouse if there is one. If there is no spouse, then equally to all living children. If there are no children, then equally to living parents. If there are no parents, then equally to living grandchildren. If there are no grandchildren, then equally to living grandparents. However, if there is a surviving spouse and the firefighter also has minor children who are not the spouse's natural or adopted children, then half the payment goes to the spouse and half is split equally among those minor children. If a firefighter dies before filing a claim, eligible family members can still file for payment. The Secretary must also provide medical services, equipment, and supplies recommended by doctors for treating these illnesses. This includes covering necessary transportation costs to get these medical services. Benefits start from the date when someone files their claim. A military firefighter means any Armed Forces member or Defense Department civilian employee whose main job is fighting and preventing fires. Congress authorizes whatever money is needed to pay for this program.
